headline: The Nez Perce Reach Bear Paw, One Day From Freedom, 1877
body:
The long journey of the Nez Perce, a flight for freedom spanning over a thousand miles, neared its bitter end in the late days of September 1877. For months, these resilient people, numbering some eight hundred souls with fewer than two hundred warriors, had outmaneuvered and outfought the United States Army across vast stretches of Idaho, Wyoming, and Montana Territories. Their destination, the sanctuary of Canada, lay tantalizingly close, a mere forty-two miles to the north. Yet, weariness, both of spirit and of flesh, began to weigh heavily upon them. The horses, their tireless companions, were spent, their strength ebbing with each passing mile.

Following the fierce engagement at Canyon Creek, a debate arose among the Nez Perce leaders. Looking Glass, a respected military strategist, advocated for a slower pace, believing the pursuing forces of General Oliver Howard to be far behind. He sought to grant his people and their mounts a much-needed respite. Poker Joe, or Lean Elk, a man of mixed blood with a keen understanding of the white man's relentless pursuit, urged haste, sensing the imminent danger. In a council held on September 24, Poker Joe, with a heavy heart, yielded to Looking Glass, reportedly stating, "Looking Glass, you can lead. I am trying to save the people, doing my best to cross into Canada before the soldiers find us. You can take command, but I think we will be caught and killed." His words, prescient and somber, foreshadowed the tragedy to come. Looking Glass assumed command, and the Nez Perce, trusting in his judgment, continued their northward trek at a deliberate, measured pace for the next four days.

Unbeknownst to them, a new threat was rapidly closing in. Colonel Nelson A. Miles, with a force of five hundred and twenty soldiers, civilian employees, and a contingent of thirty Cheyenne and Lakota scouts, had departed Fort Keogh on September 18. Miles, a determined and swift commander, was pressing hard from the southeast. On September 29, a chilling blanket of snow descended upon the landscape, marking the fateful day. Miles's Cheyenne scouts, keen-eyed and relentless, discovered the Nez Perce trail, leading to a brief skirmish with Nez Perce warriors. That same day, Nez Perce scouts reported the presence of a large body of people to their east. Yet, Looking Glass, clinging to the hope of safety, dismissed these sightings as other Indian hunting parties, perhaps Assiniboine or Gros Ventre. This misjudgment, born of exhaustion and a desperate longing for peace, proved to be a critical error.

Thus, on September 29, 1877, the Nez Perce made camp on Snake Creek, believing themselves to be within reach of freedom, unaware of the storm gathering on their eastern horizon. They prepared to resume their journey slowly the following morning, a journey that would be brutally interrupted by Miles's dawn attack on September 30. The words of Chief Joseph, spoken days later in surrender, would echo the profound sorrow of this moment: "From where the sun now stands, I will fight no more forever."
